coredata.dc:title: A design of red emission CDs-based aptasensor for sensitive detection of insulin via fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yanhua
Cheng, Yongsheng
Wen, Xiaoye
coredata.dc:description: We successfully designed an aptasensor based on the red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) and effectively detected insulin (INS) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the process, the aptamer (apt) labeled with R-CDs (R-CDs@apt) was used as fluorescence donor and graphene oxide (GO) was used as fluorescence receptor. The successful detection due to the aptamer sequence has a certain affinity for Go and INS, while the affinity for INS is stronger than that of GO. When INS is not added to the detection system, the aptamer is adsorbed onto the surface of GO, shortening the distance between R-CDs@apt and GO, resulting in FRET and the quenching of fluorescence of R-CDs@apt. When INS was added to the detection system, the aptamer selectively bound INS and separated from the adsorption of GO, FRET gradually disappeared and the fluorescence of R-CDs@apt/GO/INS system was restored. By comparing the changes of fluorescence intensity before and after adding INS, the detection of INS was implemented. The aptasensor has a good linear curve with the detection limit of as low as 1.1 nM when the concentration of INS reached 1.3–150 nM. This method has excellent selectivity and anti-interference. Therefore, it is a potential method for detecting substances in biological fluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared emission carbon dots
Graphene oxide
Aptasensor
Insulin
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectral characterization of R-CDs and R-CDs@apt

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Project of Colleges in Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work were completed under the support of the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Colleges in Shanxi Province (2020L0233).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of R-CDs; Inset: particle size distribution of R-CDs; (B) UV–visible absorption spectrum and emission spectra of R-CDs; (C) TEM image of GO; (D) FT-IR spectra of GO.
(A) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs@apt. (B) UV–visible absorption spectrum of R-CDs and R-CDs@apt. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs@apt.
TEM image of R- CDs@apt adsorbed to the surface of GO.
Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs@apt (a), R-CDs@apt/GO (b) and R-CDs@apt/GO/INS (c).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the aptasensor we developed with various concentrations of insulin; (B) The linear relationship of the aptasensor we developed with various concentrations of insulin.
Sensing mechanism of the FRET aptsensor for INS detection.
Recovery of the detection system in fetal bovine serum samples.
Comparison of the performance of INS detection using aptamers as recognition elements published in recent years.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The symbol ‘_’ suggests that it was not possible to clearly state the result in the article.

head.ce:title: A design of red emission CDs-based aptasensor for sensitive detection of insulin via fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensing mechanism of the FRET aptsensor for INS detection.

We successfully designed an aptasensor based on the red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) and effectively detected insulin (INS) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the process, the aptamer (apt) labeled with R-CDs (R-CDs@apt) was used as fluorescence donor and graphene oxide (GO) was used as fluorescence receptor. The successful detection due to the aptamer sequence has a certain affinity for Go and INS, while the affinity for INS is stronger than that of GO. When INS is not added to the detection system, the aptamer is adsorbed onto the surface of GO, shortening the distance between R-CDs@apt and GO, resulting in FRET and the quenching of fluorescence of R-CDs@apt. When INS was added to the detection system, the aptamer selectively bound INS and separated from the adsorption of GO, FRET gradually disappeared and the fluorescence of R-CDs@apt/GO/INS system was restored. By comparing the changes of fluorescence intensity before and after adding INS, the detection of INS was implemented. The aptasensor has a good linear curve with the detection limit of as low as 1.1 nM when the concentration of INS reached 1.3–150 nM. This method has excellent selectivity and anti-interference. Therefore, it is a potential method for detecting substances in biological fluids.

ce:section.ce:para: The level of blood glucose concentration is closely related to human health. High blood glucose concentration will damage human eyes, kidneys, nerves, heart and other organs [1], while low blood glucose will lead to symptoms such as hunger, sweating, panic, trembling, pale face and even coma [2]. Insulin (INS) plays an important role in controlling and regulating blood glucose level. As the only hormone in organisms, it can reduce blood glucose concentration and promote glycogen, fat and protein synthesis [3]. Therefore, INS is usually used as a marker for glucose concentration forecast in body fluids [4]. Despite the development of electrochemistry [5], photochemistry [6], high performance liquid chromatography [7], Raman light scattering [8] and other methods for INS detection, these methods with certain defects can hardly meet the needs in the field of medical detection [9]. It is reported that the incidence rate of chronic diseases caused by abnormal secretion of INS levels is increasing year by year, posing a great threat to human health [10]. Therefore, it is very important to develop a sensitive and efficient method for INS detection.

The fluorescent sensor detection method using aptamer (apt) as biometric element is widely used in the field of biomedical detection for its high stability, sensitivity, simple operation and convenience [11-13]. Apt is a single stranded DNA (s DNA) or RNA sequence in which its secondary or tertiary structure is screened and synthesized by biological in vitro exponential enrichment (SELEX) system, with the advantages of good stability, biocompatibility, easy surface modification as well as low price [14-16].

In 2009, Kazunori Ikebukuro et al. [17] screened an oligonucleotide aptamer sequence with high affinity for INS at room temperature. Subsequently, by combining it with CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [18] and fluorescent dyes FAM[19], researchers prepared fluorescent aptasensors and realized the detection of INS. However, the excitation wavelength of fluorescent probes used in these methods is mostly within the ultraviolet/visible range, which is easily disturbed by spontaneous fluorescence and scattered light. Cai et al. [20] coupled apt as a fluorescent probe using near infrared quantum dots (CdTeSe/ZnS), and performed the sensitive detection of INS in human plasma. The experiment resultsproved that the near-infrared emission fluorescent probes can well avoid the noise interference of endogenous substances (serum protein, hemoglobin, water, bilirubin and other substances) in biological tissues [21]. However, the process of preparing heavy metal quantum dots is complex, easily causing secondary pollution to biological fluid during the detection.

Red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) can address above problems not only for the ability to avoid the interference of spontaneous fluorescence and scattered light, but also due to the low cytotoxicity [22-24]. In particular, capable of deeply penetrating into biological tissue with little damage to biological tissue, it is favored by medical testers [22,25]. Graphene oxide (Go) is an excellent carbon-based fluorescence quencher with a large specific surface area, good water dispersion, easy modification, high stability, high yield, easy large-scale production, wide-absorbing spectrum range and remote quenching effect. Compared with other fluorescence quenchers, it has better stability and biocompatibility in biological liquids [26,27].

Here, we chose R-CDs as the fluorescent probe. INS aptamer (apt) was modified on its surface to prepare R-CDs@Apt as the fluorescent donor, and GO was used as the fluorescent receptor to prepare FRET biosensor (R-CDS@apt/GO). With the help of the strong π - π force between GO and apt in the free state, the distance between R-CDS@Apt and GO was shortened, FRET occurred and the fluorescence of R-CDS@Apt/GO system was quenched. After INS was added to the sensing system, apt selectively bound with INS and separated from GO. FRET disappeared, and the fluorescence of the detection system gradually recovered to realize the detection of INS.

Fetal Bovine Serum, Glucose (Glu), Adrenaline (Adr), prostate specific antigen (PSA), Uric acid (UA) and immunoglobulin G (IgG) were purchased from Yuan Ye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) were purchased from Suzhou Xing shuo Nano Technology Co., Ltd （Suzhou, China), and they were solvothermally synthesized. NaCl, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, and all the other reagents are analytical grade, which were bought from Guangfu Chemical Corporation (Tianjin, China). Amino modified and HPLC purified INS aptamers : (5′-（CH2）6-NH 2 GGT GGT GGG GGG GGT TGG TAG GGT GTC TTC-3′(MW:9672.35) [16] were synthesized in Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) and guarded with TE buffer placed at −20 ◦C refrigerator before use.1-Ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and Nhydroxysuccinimide ester (NHS) were bought from Aladdin Limited Company. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis bags (MW = 7000) were offered by Union Carbide Company. (USA). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was adopted as the washing and binding buffer. In the Milliq purification system, the reagent was diluted with ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ. cm).

R-CDs, R- CDs@apt and GO were characterized using a variety of technologies and the fluorescence of the detection system was measured. The description of the instrument and measurement conditions can be found in the supplementary materials.
The GO in this paper was synthesized based on the improved Hummers’ method [26]. Detailed preparation methods can be found in the supporting materials.

The –COOH of R-CDs (1 mg/mL, 1 mL) was firstly inspired by EDC (20 mM, 1 mL) and NHS (200 mM, 1 mL) in PBS (pH 7.4) for 2 h. 30 μg (1 OD) of aptamers were added suddenly, and the mixture was subsequently incubated at 4 °C for 24 h. Aptamer-modified R-CDs (R-CDs@apt) were gathered in a dialysis bag (MW = 7000) till there was no UV–visible absorption or fluorescence emission in the dialysate. The dialysate was kept for use at 4 ℃.

INS detection involved two steps: fluorescence “turn off” stage and fluorescence “turn on” stage. Both reactions occurred in a 10 mL colorimetric tube. The specific process is as follows: at the fluorescence “turn off” stage, 7 μg/mL R-CDs@apt and a variety of various concentrations of GO were successively put into the colorimetric tube. PBS (pH 7.4) was put into the reaction solution till the final volume reached 5 mL and it was shaken evenly. The fluorescence intensity was measured after incubation at room temperature for 15 min. The optimal concentration of GO was obtained through optimization. At the fluorescence “turn on” stage, under the optimum reaction concentration, R-CDs@apt (35 μg/mL, 1 mL), GO (10 mg/mL, 30 μL) and a variety of various concentrations of INS (1.3–150 nM) were put into the colorimetric tube in sequence, and the final volume of 5 mL was added to PBS (pH 7.4), which was mixed evenly and reacted for 30 min at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity of the reaction solution was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 560 nm. The slit width for the excitation and emission was 10 nm. In order to avoid background interference, the relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 − 1 was used to represent the fluorescence output intensity, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity when INS was not present in the detection system and when INS was present respectively.

In order to test the practicability of the detection method, fetal bovine serum was diluted 10 times by PBS (pH 7.4) as the reaction solution. After different concentrations of INS was added, we tested the fluorescence intensity, compared with the standard curve, and calculated the recovery rate.

As shown in the Fig. S2A the Raman spectra of R-CDs have characteristic peaks at 1360 (sp3 hybrid carbon) and 1596 (sp2 hybrid carbon), respectively [28]. Fig. S2B depicts the XRD crystal profile of the carbon dots, with a broad peak at 24.5°, which indicated the planar and amorphous crystalline nature of the carbon dots [29]. From the HRTEM image of R-CDs (Fig. 1 A), we can see that the R-CDs are uniform and the average crystalline size is calculated as 4.1 nm. R-CDs’ UV absorption is the strongest at about 560 nm (Fig. 1B). Under this excitation, the emission spectrum range of R-CDs was 610–640 nm, located in the red emission region, which could well avoid the noise interference of endogenous substances in biological fluids [30-33]. Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, we used 560 nm as the excitation spectrum of R-CDs for detection.

The XRD spectrum of GO shows a characteristic peak at 11.6°(Fig.S2C). Fig. 1C shows the TEM image of GO, and the results show that we have successfully oxidized graphite powder to GO with a thickness of about 2 nm [32]. The FT-IR spectra of GO (Fig. 1D) shows that this GO has stretching peaks at 3413 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, 1398 cm−1, 1225 cm−1 and 1052 cm−1, respectively, indicating that the GO includes a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups and has good dispersion in water, and it also provides conditions for π - π interacting with aptamers [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of R-CDs and R-CDs@apt
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 2 A, FT-IR spectra of R-CDs illustrates that there are a lot of carboxyl groups like O−H at 3394 cm−1 and 1388 cm−1, C-H at 2921 cm−1, C-OH at 1708 cm−1 on the surface of R-CDs [33], which is very beneficial for reacting with modified amino groups on aptamers. From the FT-IR spectra of R- CDs@apt, it can be seen that new stretching peaks appear at positions: 2969 cm−1, 1648 cm−1, 1563 cm−1, and 1469 cm−1, indicating the bond of N-H, N-C=O, C=N and C-N. These results suggest that amino and carboxyl groups have reacted to form peptide bonds [34]. From Fig. 2b, a new absorption peak appears for R-CDs@apt around 260 nm when comparing R-CDs with R-CDs@apt, indicating the occurrence of the coupling reaction between R-CDs and aptamer [34,35]. After the coupling reaction, the excess R-CDs are removed by dialysis, so the fluorescence intensity of R- CDs@apt is slightly lower than that of R-CDs, but the position of fluorescence emission spectrum remains unchanged (peak value is 625 nm) (Fig. 2C). Graphene oxide (GO) can absorb all lights from all visible regions to near-infrared regions [36], which is the premise of FRET between R- CDs@apt and GO.

FRET occurred. The fluorescence of R-CDs@apt was turned off (compare curves a and b in Fig. 4 ); However, when INS was put into the detection system, the aptamer sequence selectively bound INS and formed a G tetrad with multiple antiparallel structures [37], hiding the hydrogen bond on the surface, thus losing the π-π adsorption force on the surface of Go, and the degree of FRET was weakened. The fluorescence of R-CDs@apt/Go/INS system gradually turned on (curve c in Fig. 4), and degree of fluorescence turn on was directly proportional to the amount of insulin added to the detection system, so as to realize INS detection.

In this study, the pH and incubation time were optimized to achieve excellent detection by modifying the aptamer, R-CDs@apt, and GO concentrations. A fluorescence quenching stage (time 1) and recovery stage (time 2) were also considered. Fig.S1.(A) shows that at the coupling stage, the F/F0 − 1 value increases with the increase of the aptamer concentration until the aptamer concentration reaches 13 ug/mL, and the excessive aptamer sequences are intertwined and the hydrogen bond is hidden, thereby reducing the binding efficiency with GO[38-41], and the value of F/F0 − 1 decreases. Therefore, 13 ug/mL was selected as the optimal concentration of the aptamer required by the coupling system. Fig. S1B shows that prolonged incubation time resulted in saturation in the reaction between R-CDs and apt. F/F0 − 1 gradually increased up to a reaction time of 24 h, which was used as the optimized incubation time.

At the coupling stage, through the adoption of strategy of excessive R-CDs , the unmodified R-CDs were removed at the end of the coupling reaction. Therefore, at the detection stage, the concentration of the coupled aptamer was used to represent the concentration of R-CDs@apt. From the Fig.S1(C), it is seen that the value of F/F0 − 1 gradually increases with the increase of R-CDs@apt concentration until the concentration reaches 7 ug/mL, and then R-CDs@apt is continuously added to the detection system, self-absorption occurs between them, the background interference increases [41], and the value of F/F0-1 decreases. Therefore, 7 ug/mL was selected as the best concentration of R-CDs@apt. Fig.S1(D) shows that the reaction effect is the optimal when the concentration of GO is 60 ug/mL. The F/F0-1 value maximized at physiological pH (7.4), due to superior aptamer activity at this pH (Fig. S1E).

As we can see from Fig.S1(F), when time (a) reaches 15 min and time (b) reaches 30 min, the reaction effect is the best, and the value of F/F0-1 reaches the peak. Therefore, we chose 15 min and 30 min as the best time of fluorescence “turn off” stage and fluorescence “turn on” stage respectively.

Under optimal experimental conditions, we investigated the sensitivity of this system to the quantitative detection of INS. From the Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs@apt/GO/INS system exhibited an increasing trend with the increase of INS concentration (1.3, 3, 5, 10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 120, 140 and 150 nM). In the concentration range of 1.3–10 nM, a clear linearity was seen, and the linear equation was F/F0 − 1 = 0.0532C[INS] + 0.0109 (R2 = 0.991). Whereas, with the increase of INS concentration, the rate of it binding to aptamer decreased, so another linear fitting curve: F/F0 − 1 = 0.0141C[INS] + 0.4799 (R2 = 0.996) appeared at the concentration of 10–150 nM (Fig. 5B.) The detection limit was 1.1 nM, counted from the equation LOD = 3σ/s, where σrepresented the standard deviation of 11 parallel measurements in the absence of INS in the detection system, and s represented the slope of the calibration curve.

To check the selectivity of this detection system, we added 5 nM INS and 10 times of other substances that may exist in biological fluids: Glucose (Glu), Adrenaline (Adr), prostate specific antigen (PSA), Uric acid (UA) and immunoglobulin G (IgG). As shown in Fig. S2A, these potential interfering substances had no significant effect on the selectivity of the detection system. The fluorescence intensity of the reaction solution after adding these substances was basically the same as that of the PBS buffer only system. We also investigated the possible interference (Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, CO3 2− and SO4 2−) in biological fluids. After 5 nM and 100 nM interference ions were added, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system varied slightly (Fig. S2B), within the allowable error range (≤5%).These results show that the method we developed has good selectivity and anti-interference for realizing INS detection.

As shown in Table 1 , three different concentrations of INS (3, 50, 120 nM) were added to the diluted fetal bovine serum. We recorded the fluorescence intensity and calculated the spiked recovery, which were 97.6%, 101.3% and 98.7% respectively, indicating that the practicability of the detection system is good.

As shown in Table 2 , the detection strategy we developed was compared with the method of INS detection using aptamer as recognition elements published in recent years. Our strategy has the advantages of low detection limit and wide linear range except for electrochemical detection methods [42,44]. However, the electrochemical sensor is easily hindered by the slow oxidation of interfering molecules (glucose, uric acid and ascorbic acid) in biological fluids, thus limiting its stability [9]. In immunoassay, the enzyme is prone to cross-reaction with other proteins and C-peptide, affecting the effect of on-site detection [47]. Moreover, the cytotoxicity of the two nanomaterials (R-CDs@apt and GO) used in this method is lower than that of CdSe/ZnS QDs [18]. The synthesis method is more simple than anthracene derivative BDVAI [46] and the emission spectrum range of the fluorescent probe used in this method is within the near-infrared region, which has better anti-interference than other fluorescence detection methods [5,17,43].

Aboveall, we have prepared a near-infrared fluorescent aptasensor and successfully realized the detection of INS in biological fluids, with no need of complex preprocessing. The experimental results show that the aptasensor has good sensitivity, anti-interference, selectivity, simplicity and efficiency in INS detection. Despite the detection limit of this detection system not the lowest, we selected near-infrared carbon nano materials as fluorescent probes in the experiment, which could well avoid the interference of spontaneous fluorescence and scattered light, and it has strong biological tissue penetration and little tissue damage. The development of this detection strategy is expected to provide a basis for the further detection of INS in biological tissues, and to expand the application range of R-CDs combined with aptamers to achieve biological detection.
Yanhua He: Methodology, Supervision. Yongsheng Cheng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoye Wen: Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work were completed under the support of the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Colleges in Shanxi Province (2020L0233).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121497.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Journal of Chromatography A2020, 1615,460741.
